content:
I have few memory mapped peripheral devices, and also have c struct definitions for these devices, they look something like this.

regs {
    union {
        struct {
            uint32_t field_a       : 1
            uint32_t field_a       : 1
            uint32_t field_c       : 30
        } BF;
        uint32_t WORD;
    } reg1;
    ...
}
typedef volatile struct regs regs_t

Using this I can read/write peripheral configuration via struct fields without doing any pointer arithmetic. Is there a equivalent way of doing things using rust in the kernel? right now I'm just using the IoMem wrapper and manually calculating offsets.
Get a raw pointer to the field using the addr_of! or addr_of_mut! macro.
Not sure what this IoMem thing is.

This iomem wrapper, it just calls out to the kernel to map a peripheral address range to virtual memory and provides readl/writel/...
What I'm doing right now is this,

let regs = unsafe { IoMem::<100>::try_new(res) }?;
let reg1 = regs.readl(0x20)
regs.writel(reg1 & 0xFF, 0x20)

What I would like to do is

let regs: MyReg = unsafe { IoMem::<100>::try_new(res) }?;
regs.reg_at_0x20 &= 0xFF;

Or even better with bitfields

let regs: MyReg = unsafe { IoMem::<100>::try_new(res) }?;
regs.reg_at_0x20.bits_0_to_7 = 0;
We don't have typed register access in the kernel and neither in the old rust branch. There are many examples of how to implement such things in the embedded Rust community though

You can do some pretty cool things like type checking that when you write value, say, CLOCK_DIVIDER_5, to a register, it will fail to type check if you write it to the worn field of the register for instance. And you can do automatic bit shifts of fields, etc.
I have it on my todo to look for a solution for the kernel to use in the NVMe driver. It would be super cool if you could come up with something :+1:
I think the stm32 rust HAL crates have typed register access https://github.com/stm32-rs
I was looking tough some embedded stuff but didn't come across the stm32 crates, I'll take a look at how they handle it. thank you :)

I just asked a friend who work with the stm32 MCUs, he pointed me to this GitHub repo: https://github.com/embassy-rs/stm32-data . They generate structs from some register definitions and those structs allow typed access to the registers
You're talking about accessing peripheral registers? The PACs usually come from svd2rust https://docs.rs/svd2rust/latest/svd2rust/
Then the HALs are an abstraction layer on top of those

I had a look at it and I have a few issues,
1) I don't have the svd files for these peripherals, and looks like creating them is a lot of work. I would much rather write down some rust code than xml .
2) They also use vcell for volatile reads/writes to structs, can I use third party creates in my own kernel module? looks like the answer might be no.
3) Rust doesn't support bitfields structs, so might need to use a third party crate for that too. for register read writes I can get around this by doing some bit shifting, but for the DMA protocol devices uses, I have to generate bitfield structs. I'm now stuck here.

They also use vcell for volatile reads/writes to structs, can I use third party creates in my own kernel module? looks like the answer might be no.

Please see https://rust-for-linux.com/third-party-crates.

vcell is a tiny crate, so it is not a very big issue -- you could even just copy-paste the code (for prototyping purposes).
Thank you, I will just copy paste the code for now. Hopefully there is a small enough bitfield crate too.

I understand that you might not be able to copy/paste code from stm32 or other embedded crates directly, but I think the concepts they use to describe memory mapped registers are really interesting, and we should try to use them in the kernel if possible

I'm not familiar with SVD and I don't know if it would make sense for us to use. But in general I think it makes sense to describe register formats and allowed values in an intermediate format and generate code from that
It looks like they are generating some json from the SVD files and going to Rust from there: https://github.com/embassy-rs/stm32-data-generated/blob/main/data/registers/adc_f3.json
In any case, they must have some concepts for the final Rust code that they generate that we might be able to reuse, if using an intermediate representation is not cool
An example of the final output is here: https://github.com/embassy-rs/stm32-data-generated/blob/main/stm32-metapac/src/registers/adc_f3.rs
We could start by handcoding stuff like that

SVD are definitely not handwritten, usually you get them from the manufacturer. Usually you can get them for embedded processor (have to chase them down a bit), I'm not sure whether you can get them for desktop processors. Maybe if you ask? It definitely makes register related things easier
For example, I know of this repo that collects embedded SVD: https://github.com/cmsis-svd/cmsis-svd/tree/main/data
Notice that writel may not be a simple write_volatile, you may need to open-code VolatileCell anyway.
https://lwn.net/Articles/698014/

In this case I will have to hand write the svd files because I'm trying to write a wifi driver using reverse engineered register definitions. we actually reached out to the manufacturer and they said they will not provide any register level definitions/docs at all.

About writel I did not have any issues using the writel function provided by IoMem wrapper so far.
I don't know whether it's better to handwrite the SVD file (probably more reusable, but means we have to have svd2rust as part of the tree) or just emulate its output handwritten
You could probably make the second case easier with some macros

I was thinking of something like this, but I have not written any macros before so I'm not sure if it's possible.

#[derive(IoMem)]
struct Regs {
    reg1: struct Reg1 {
         #[bf(bits=0..15)]
         field1: u32
         #[bf(bits=15..20)]
         field2: u32
         #[bf(bits=20..31)]
         field3: u32
    }
    reg2: struct Reg2 {...
}

then the macro can generate actual structs for each registers and define operations on each field. maybe something like
regs.reg1.field1.write(value). I would like to do regs.reg1.field1 = value but looks like this may not be possible with rust semantics today.

The PAC crates give you a read function that can access each bit, then a write function that takes a closure so you can set individual bits. I find it pretty elegant but I'm sure the C people would go bleh
https://docs.rust-embedded.org/book/start/registers.html#reading

You could likely create the same sort of thing with a rules macro I think

define_registers! {
    ctl: u32 {
        // name: access, bits, repr, bit-offset
        // I don't think a derive macro could process a `..` range since it sees that as a single token
        field1: RW, 0:2, u8;
        field2: R, 2:16, u8;
        field3: W, 16:171, bool,;
    }
}

Ugh, then again maybe it is easier to just handwrite a SVD file and see what the tool spits out. Probably just as much work with less Rust to maintain, better code (the output from svd2rust is really pretty bulletproof), and the C side could use it too.

Or maybe you don't need anything special at all for a small here, I don't know how many mmio registers you are dealing with.

I guess it could depend on whether using svd2rust in the toolchain or having files produced by svd2rust would be acceptable (@Miguel Ojeda)
After all, using SVD files is common in C embedded toolchains too. They're one way to interface custom FPGA peripherals your code running on a core

I highly doubt kernel people would like SVD. If I understand correctly:

  • It's format by ARM, largely intended just for ARM embedded systems (although I do see some RISC-V projects adopting it)
  • CMSIS is licensed in Apache and is not compatible with GPL-2
  • It's XML, so maintainability is questionable
  • Adding svd2rust or C equivalent will add another build dependency to the kernel.

I think we'd be better off with just declarative/procedural macros.

Gary Guo said:

I highly doubt kernel people would like SVD. If I understand correctly:

  • It's format by ARM, largely intended just for ARM embedded systems (although I do see some RISC-V projects adopting it)
  • CMSIS is licensed in Apache and is not compatible with GPL-2
  • It's XML, so maintainability is questionable
  • Adding svd2rust or C equivalent will add another build dependency to the kernel.

I think we'd be better off with just declarative/procedural macros.

I don't know if SVD is usable or not, but the whole concept is one we should investigate for use in the kernel. We should have some kind of intermediate format that describes layout of a block of registers, the fields of those registers, and the allowed values of those fields along with names for these values. It would probably make sense to allow encoding of some kind of documentation as well.

Regarding build time dependencies, I would imagine we would check in the generated code. You would only need the dependency if you wanted to add new types.

I think depending in macros for this is a bad idea, because they are not easily maintained. They can especially be difficult to understand when written without access to crates like syn. An external tool would be far simpler to maintain.

I know that some work is going on to do a similar thing for NVMe, and from what I hear, block layer subsystem is very interested in that work. This work would also rely on third party tools. In fact we should probably sync up with the people who are doing that work, since it is sort of similar.

Ah thanks for the info Gary. It is kind of unfortunate because SVD is kind of a standard format for every bit of information we need (peripheral<-register<-field, addresses, documentation, R/W/RE, reset values, peripheral class) and that’s a lot of information to encode in a nonstandard format like a macro.

Maybe we could write a macro that takes the same inputs as SVD and produces the same outputs as svd2rust? So we don’t have to reinvent the wheel on both the information and the generated typesafe format.
It seems that the embassy generated PAC comes from yaml data. They do a one time import of the SVD and convert it to yaml. Then they touch up the yaml by hand.
Like so: https://github.com/embassy-rs/stm32-data/blob/main/data/registers/adc_f1.yaml

That seems equally reasonable. If you did json instead, you could probably do the rust codegen with a python script and sidestep the extra system / cargo dependencies (same for XML but ET is definitely harder to work with. Though maybe not when attributes aren’t in use…?)

I guess you could really go about this in a lot of different ways. Keeping input as something along the lines of SVD seems reasonable since it’s well proven, even if you change the file format or just put it directly into a macro. Lots of paths to get from there to useable Rust

@Trevor Gross @Madushan Nishantha I hacked together a quick PoC based on the embassy stm32 stuff. See latest commit here for the generated output used in the NVMe Rust driver and see here for the yaml input for generating the code.
That looks pretty great to me
Well, it looks like chiptool does the generation and it’s just a fork of svd2rust that works on yaml?
Seems like this does the heavy lifting https://github.com/embassy-rs/chiptool
That looks pretty good to me too.

Trevor Gross said:

Well, it looks like chiptool does the generation and it’s just a fork of svd2rust that works on yaml?

Yes, I just put the binary in the stm32-data crate because I was lazy and that is what I used as a reference. From the readme it looks like the embassy people forked svd2rust to change a number of thinks that would make it align better with their requirements.
I think that with a workflow like this, we could check in the generated code and depend on upstream chiptool. We an always fork it if we need to. I am not sure about how licencing would work.
I think checking in the generated code is probably okay, if AMD can do it, why can't we.
I came across this crate which provides good abstractions for bitfields https://crates.io/crates/modular-bitfield
and this https://github.com/hecatia-elegua/bilge
I think what chiptool generates should be enough? It already handles access to bitfields, enums for values, defaults

I was looking at these because the device I'm working with requires generating packed structs for the dma protocol. register fields and packed struct fields look similar enough so maybe it makes sense to use the same strategy to do both?
You mean for writing dma descriptors in memory buffers?

It could be used, as long as you don't have fields that span the architecture word boundary, and as long as the fields of the structure does not change depending om some discriminant somewhere in the structure. For the same reason, the chiptool generated code does not work well with banked or shadowed registers.

At least for NVMe we need something else for writing entries to the IO queues. The entries written to the queues can be shaped differently depending on what kind of request we are issuing or completing.
hmm the dma headers are definitely not byte aligned for this device. (there is one that's 68 bits long).
I am curious what device are you working with?
bl808, but the device has a bunch of dma controllers and I'm trying to setup the one specific to wifi controller
Ok, cool. Are you using other code as a reference? I just checked out the reference manual, and there is no mention of the wifi block :grimacing:

When you say DMA headers, what do you mean exactly? Is this a register in the DMA peripheral, or do you write DMA commands to a piece of system memory and then instruct the DMA controller to fetch commands from there? Or are you talking about the actual payload that is transferred to/from the WiFi peripheral?
oh, yeah, no docs on wifi. I'm revere engineering the interface

the way it operates is, I have to setup a circular linked list with a specific header format and tell the dma engine where the head of that list is. then dma engine will either read or write the payload based on the entries in the header linked list. It's operation is similar to the dma engine documented in the bl808 manual.

I see. It looks like the list entries always have the same layout and have only a few fields. You could just hand code the access methods for those fields, it would not be a lot. You could use chiptool to generate the code as well, but as far as I know that tool does not support fields larger than 64 bits. But I could be wrong. Both of the bit field crates you linked could be used as well, if they support this kind of 68 bit wide fields.
The benefit of using chiptool or just handwriting the code, is that you are not going to depend on a 3rd party crate that you have to fight for to get into the kernel
I am curious what field in this DMA engine is going to be 68 bits wide?
Pointers on this platform are probably 32 or 64 bits right?

The header for wifi dma is slightly different from what's in the datasheet, it has flags related to rf stuff. that fields ends up being 68 bits. I'm not exactly sure how it works on the peripheral side without being properly aligned either. and yeah pointers are 64 bits in linux. (but the blob I'm revere engineering is 32 bits, runs in a different core)
So there is a bitfield in this 68 bit thing that crosses a 8 byte boundary? It sounds strange, but you never know what people design :sweat_smile:
It would make more sense to leave a few reserved bits in one word and move the bitfield to start on an alligned boundary

Madushan Nishantha said:

The header for wifi dma is slightly different from what's in the datasheet, it has flags related to rf stuff. that fields ends up being 68 bits. I'm not exactly sure how it works on the peripheral side without being properly aligned either. and yeah pointers are 64 bits in linux. (but the blob I'm revere engineering is 32 bits, runs in a different core)

I can't see the layout of the actual list nodes in the reference manual anyway. It leaves a lot of information out, it seems.
yeah most of the operation mode of most peripherals on this device we have to read the c sdk to figure out. manual is not that great.

I figured out there is another way to work with bitfields by just including the c-header and then use the api generated by bindgen. but I don't like it that much https://rust-lang.github.io/rust-bindgen/using-bitfields.html
but since it's already there maybe I can write a macro to to generate field accessoros using __BindgenBitfieldUnit type
Yes, you can use the bindgen generated code to set the bitfields. I think we are generating those methods. But you will not get typed access to the fields, they will just be numbers.
for now I just added bilge crate to my code for packed struct support. I will refactor to whatever the kernel people settle on when the time comes

I'm currently working on a abstraction for regmap. This is still in early stage and the code will probably change daily, but at least the basics are working: https://github.com/Fabo/linux/commits/fparent/rust-regmap-dev.

You can check some usage example here: https://github.com/Fabo/linux/commit/68467da584b24457f7b0d7ffe6501995bcaed2ca
